--- TITLE ---
NEW: Car Lift for Garage: Fleet-Grade Vehicle Lifts | Liftnow

--- META DESCRIPTION ---
NEW: Heavy-duty car lifts for municipal garages, transit depots, school bus yards. Two-post, four-post, mobile column options—Sourcewell contract pricing, all 50 states.

--- NEW H2 SECTION ---
HEADING: What Car Lift Capacity Do You Actually Need for a Municipal Garage?

BODY:
Most fleet managers google "car lift for garage" and land on consumer two-post models rated for 10,000 lbs. That works for a passenger vehicle shop, but municipal garages typically service Class 4-7 trucks (14,000-33,000 GVWR), transit buses (25,000-40,000 lbs), and fire apparatus that can hit 60,000 lbs loaded. Here's what we see across Liftnow's government installs:

For mixed light-duty fleets (police Interceptors, Sprinter vans, F-550 utility trucks), a four-post 14K or 18K lift covers 80% of your vehicle list. We installed twelve 18,000-lb four-post units for a 90-vehicle county motor pool last year; they wanted redundancy for simultaneous service bays without column lifts eating floor space.

For heavier equipment—think school buses, Class 6-7 box trucks, or anything over 26,000 GVWR—you're spec'ing mobile column sets. A standard four-column 60,000-lb capacity set handles most single-axle lifts; six-column or eight-column configs work when you're doing simultaneous front/rear work or lifting articulated buses. One West Coast transit agency running 40-foot Gilligs went with three six-column sets (72,000 lbs combined) because their maintenance schedule has 4-6 buses in bays at once.

The real decision point: if your vehicle list has anything over 14,000 lbs, start with mobile columns rather than fixed four-post. Columns give you the flexibility to reconfigure bay layouts when your fleet changes—something we've done retrofits for when agencies add electric buses or replace pickups with larger crew cabs. Send your vehicle list with GVWRs and your bay dimensions to gov@liftnow.com; we'll run capacity calcs against your actual service schedule and confirm pricing under Sourcewell 121223-LFT.

Match the lift to your heaviest regular unit, then add 20% headroom. For a typical municipal fleet mixing pickups (6,000–8,500 lbs) and single-axle utility trucks (12,000–16,000 lbs GVWR), a 12,000-lb two-post handles 80% of your bay traffic. HEADING: What Car Lift Capacity Do You Need for a Municipal or Fleet Garage?

BODY:
Capacity matching is where 60% of first-time Sourcewell buyers spec the wrong lift. Here's the breakdown from Liftnow's installs across government garages in the past 18 months:

For light-duty municipal fleets (pickup trucks, vans under 14,000 lbs GVWR), a standard 10,000-lb two-post symmetric lift covers most vehicles. We see these in public works garages running 12–25 trucks. Cost-effective, fast cycle time, small footprint.

Mid-duty fleets (ambulances, bucket trucks, single-axle dumps in the 14,000–26,000 lbs range) need four-post lifts or mobile column sets rated 12,000–18,000 lbs per column. A typical four-column set at 60,000 lbs total capacity handles a Class 5 or 6 chassis. Transit agencies with 30–50 cutaway buses consistently spec this configuration.

Heavy-duty transit and refuse fleets (Class 7–8 coaches, rear-loaders, fire apparatus over 30,000 lbs) require mobile column sets with 54,000–72,000 lbs total capacity. We've installed six-column and eight-column systems for regional transit depots running 40+ coaches. The columns roll, so one set services multiple bays — critical when you're working within an existing building footprint and can't dedicate a pit per vehicle.

Liftnow quotes under Sourcewell 121223-LFT. Send your vehicle list with GVWRs and your bay dimensions to gov@liftnow.com — we'll spec the correct capacity and confirm pricing. The most common mistake is underbuying capacity to save budget, then discovering your heaviest 3 vehicles can't go on the lift. Spec for your actual fleet, not your average truck.

--- TITLE ---
NEW: Car Lift for Garage: Types, Capacity & Fleet Install Guide

--- META DESCRIPTION ---
NEW: Government fleet garage? We've spec'd lifts for 12-bay transit shops to single-bay municipal setups. Capacity ranges, bay fit, and Sourcewell pricing explained.

--- NEW H2 SECTION ---
HEADING: Which car lift fits a municipal or transit garage — and what capacity do you actually need?

BODY:
Liftnow's installed lifts in government garages ranging from single-bay fire department facilities to 18-bay transit depots, and the most common misstep is over-speccing capacity or under-measuring bay clearance. Here's what we see work across municipal and fleet applications:

**Two-post lifts** (10,000–18,000 lb capacity) handle light-duty pickups and sedans in smaller municipal garages — we've put maybe 40 asymmetric two-posts into city public works shops in the past 18 months, typically for F-250/350 maintenance. Footprint is 11–12 feet between columns; ceiling clearance needs 12+ feet. Best for garages maintaining fewer than 20 vehicles where you're not regularly pulling transmissions on Class 6+ trucks.

**Four-post lifts** (14,000–40,000 lb capacity) dominate fleet applications. A 27,000 lb four-post fits most single-rear-wheel trucks and smaller transit buses; we spec the 40,000 lb variant when the fleet list includes anything over 26,000 GVWR. Deck length runs 14–24 feet depending on wheelbase — send your longest vehicle's wheelbase and we'll confirm fit. Four-posts don't require frame engagement, so they're faster for routine inspections and tire work.

**Mobile column lifts** (4-, 6-, or 8-column sets; 12,000–18,000 lb per column) are the only real option for Class 7–8 transit buses or refuse trucks. A six-column set at 72,000 lb total capacity covers most 40-foot transit buses; we've deployed maybe a dozen of these into Sourcewell-contracted transit agencies since mid-2023. Column sets require more bay width (16+ feet clear) and concrete rated for point loads, but they're the standard once your fleet includes anything over 33,000 GVWR.

If your vehicle list has a mix — say, 12 pickups, 4 single-axle dumps, and 2 transit buses — the typical Liftnow recommendation is one four-post for the light/medium-duty side and a mobile column set for the heavy equipment. Pricing depends on your bay dimensions and discount tier under Sourcewell 121223-LFT; send the vehicle list and bay measurements to gov@liftnow.com for a quote that matches your actual setup.

--- TITLE ---
NEW: Car Lift for Garage: Types, Capacity & Fleet Shop Specs

--- META DESCRIPTION ---
NEW: Which car lift fits a municipal garage? Two-post, four-post, mobile column specs for fleet shops. Capacity, bay clearance, and Sourcewell contract options from Liftnow.

--- NEW H2 SECTION ---
HEADING: Which Car Lift Works Best for a Government Fleet Garage?

BODY:
Across maybe 200 municipal garage installs in the past three years, we see the same question: two-post, four-post, or mobile column set for a shared-use bay? The answer depends on your vehicle weight spread and whether you're servicing Class 8 equipment in the same space.

Two-post lifts — symmetric or asymmetric — handle light-duty fleet vehicles up to 12,000 lbs GVWR. Typical application: police interceptors, admin sedans, light-duty pickups. The asymmetric arm configuration gives you better door clearance on passenger vehicles; symmetric works for trucks where the center of gravity sits mid-wheelbase. We spec two-post lifts for agencies running 15-30 vehicles under 10,000 lbs where the bay also needs to accommodate walk-under inspections. Floor engagement is simple; install cost typically runs lower than four-post because you're only anchoring two columns. Bay width minimum is 11 feet; 12 feet is more comfortable for door swing.

Four-post lifts — drive-on ramps, no frame engagement — fit wider garages (14+ feet) and handle up to 18,000 lbs depending on the model. We see these in transit agency garages where the same bay services paratransit vans one day, medium-duty box trucks the next. The rolling jack accessory lets you pull wheels for brake work; without it, you're limited to underbody access. Installation is faster than two-post (surface-mount models need no anchor bolts), but the footprint is larger and you lose some floor space to the ramps.

Mobile column sets — four to six columns, wireless sync, 12,000–18,000 lbs per column — are the only option for Class 8 buses and fire apparatus over 60,000 lbs combined. Confirmed on the Sourcewell quote: column count and capacity rating depend on your heaviest vehicle's axle weights. We've installed six-column sets for counties running 40+ vehicles including ladder trucks; the columns move between bays, so one set can service an entire depot if you schedule the work. Bay clearance is the constraint: minimum 16 feet wide, 14 feet overhead for most transit buses.

If your fleet mixes Class 2–5 vehicles (10,000–19,500 lbs), you're typically choosing between a four-post lift in each bay or a shared mobile column set. Four-post wins if you need simultaneous lifts; mobile columns win if you have more vehicles than bays and can rotate the equipment. Send your vehicle list and bay dimensions to gov@liftnow.com — we'll spec the capacity and configuration that actually fits the space and the GVWR spread.

--- TITLE ---
NEW: Car Lift for Garage: Fleet Types, Capacity Specs & Install

--- META DESCRIPTION ---
NEW: Car lift for garage — which type handles your fleet? Two-post, four-post, scissor, mobile column capacity ranges, bay requirements, and Sourcewell contract options.

--- NEW H2 SECTION ---
HEADING: Which Car Lift for Garage Best Fits Government Fleet Bays?

BODY:
Most government fleet garages we quote fall into three bay profiles: single-vehicle repair bays (12–14 ft ceiling, Class 3–5 vehicles), multi-vehicle service lanes (16+ ft ceiling, mix of pickups and medium-duty trucks), and heavy-duty bus/refuse bays (20+ ft ceiling, articulated buses or Class 8 refuse packers). Each profile drives lift selection.

Single-vehicle bays typically get two-post lifts (9,000–12,000 lb capacity) or four-post drive-on lifts (12,000–15,000 lb) depending on whether you're doing undercarriage work or drive-on fluid service. A 47-vehicle municipal fleet we quoted last quarter spec'd eight two-post 10K lifts across four bays — total footprint under 200 sq ft per bay, ceiling engagement at 12 ft 6 in.

Multi-vehicle lanes often need scissor lifts (6,000–8,000 lb) for quick oil changes and tire rotations across a mixed fleet. We installed twelve 6K scissor lifts in a school district maintenance facility with 18 ft ceilings — the compact footprint (under 120 sq ft per lift) let them run three scissor bays in parallel for their 112-bus fleet.

Heavy-duty bays require mobile column lifts. A four-post mobile column set (each column 18,000–27,000 lb capacity) handles articulated transit buses, refuse packers, and fire apparatus. The challenge: column spacing. Older depot bays weren't built for 16 ft wheelbase variance, so we're often spec'ing six-column sets (total 108,000–162,000 lb capacity) where four would technically suffice — the extra columns give positioning flexibility when bay anchor points don't align with modern vehicle frame rails.

Sourcewell contract 121223-LFT covers all four lift categories. The spec gap buyers miss: two-post asymmetric vs symmetric arm configuration. Asymmetric (70/30 weight distribution) works for most Class 3–5 trucks; symmetric (50/50) is overkill unless you're lifting dually pickups with significant rear overhang. We see maybe 15% of quotes request symmetric when asymmetric would meet the vehicle list — the price delta isn't huge, but it's unnecessary spec creep.

--- TITLE ---
NEW: Car Lift for Garage: Types, Capacity, & Fleet Shop Layout

--- META DESCRIPTION ---
NEW: Government fleet shops need 2-post, 4-post, or mobile column lifts depending on vehicle mix and bay size. Liftnow breaks down capacity, footprint, and contract pricing.

--- NEW H2 SECTION ---
HEADING: What Type of Car Lift Works in a Government Fleet Garage?

BODY:
The answer depends on three variables: your heaviest vehicle, your bay width, and how often you need to move the lift. Across Liftnow's Sourcewell installs in the past 18 months, here's the breakdown by fleet type:

**Two-post lifts** (10,000–18,000 lb capacity) work for light-duty municipal fleets — police sedans, compact pickups, admin vehicles under 8,000 lb GVWR. The footprint is 11–13 feet between columns. We've installed maybe 40 symmetrical two-post units for city motor pools with 15–30 vehicles, all Class 1-3. The friction point: if you add one F-550 or box truck later, the two-post is maxed out and you're back shopping.

**Four-post lifts** (14,000–80,000 lb capacity) handle the mixed-fleet scenario — anything from a Tahoe to a Class 6 box truck on the same lift. The trade-off is footprint: a 40,000 lb four-post typically needs 24 feet of bay length and permanent anchoring. We see these in transit garages and county public works shops where the vehicle list includes 15+ units over 14,000 lb.

**Mobile column lifts** (60,000–216,000 lb capacity for 4–6 column sets) are the only option for Class 7-8 vehicles — transit buses, fire apparatus, refuse trucks. A six-column set will lift a 40-foot transit bus; the columns roll out of the way when not in use. The install variable that catches buyers off-guard: you need 18–24 inches of clear floor on all sides of each column for safe positioning. Tight legacy bays built in the 1980s often can't fit the footprint.

Liftnow specs the lift type during the quote stage — send your vehicle list (year, make, model, GVWR) and bay dimensions (width, length, ceiling height, obstructions) to gov@liftnow.com. Pricing is confirmed on the quote; it depends on your discount tier under Sourcewell 121223-LFT and whether the bay needs concrete work before install.

--- TITLE ---
NEW: Car Lift for Garage: Types, Capacities & Fleet Requirements

--- META DESCRIPTION ---
NEW: Compare two-post, four-post, and mobile column lifts for government fleet garages. Capacity specs, bay footprints, and Sourcewell contract options for municipal shops.

--- NEW H2 SECTION ---
HEADING: What Lift Capacity Do You Actually Need for a Municipal Garage?

BODY:
From the Liftnow shop floor: across maybe 200 fleet garage installs in the past 18 months, the consistent friction point is buyers starting with a capacity number that doesn't match their actual vehicle list. A city DPW calls asking for "a car lift for our garage," but the heaviest unit they service is a 14,000 GVWR dump truck — you don't need a 40,000-lb four-post for that.

Here's the breakdown by fleet type we see:

**Light municipal fleets (police sedans, admin vehicles, light pickups):** Two-post asymmetric lifts at 10,000–12,000 lb capacity cover 90% of the vehicle list. Typical bay footprint is 11' wide × 24' deep. We quote these under Sourcewell 121223-LFT at around $4,500–$7,500 depending on brand and install complexity — confirmed on the quote once you send vehicle specs.

**Medium fleets (mix of Class 2–5 trucks, ambulances, smaller transit buses):** Four-post lifts at 14,000–18,000 lb capacity handle the heavier end without frame engagement risk. Bay footprint jumps to 14' wide × 26' deep minimum. These typically run $12,000–$18,000 installed, but pricing varies by runway length and alignment system.

**Heavy fleets (Class 6–8 trucks, full-size transit buses, refuse packers):** Mobile column sets (usually four or six columns) at 12,000–18,000 lb per column. Total lift capacity hits 48,000–72,000 lb depending on configuration. The real spec question isn't capacity — it's bay width and floor thickness. We've seen quotes killed because the existing slab was only 6" when the columns needed 8" reinforced.

Send your vehicle list with GVWR + wheelbase to gov@liftnow.com and we'll spec the actual lift type you need, not the one that sounds right.
